name: PCDIC
description_short: PCDIC is a community development corporation offering loans and grants to nonprofits and small businesses in underserved Phoenix and Maricopa County areas.
description_long:
The Phoenix Community Development & Investment Corporation (PCDIC) was established in 2002 by the City of Phoenix to address capital gaps in economically disadvantaged communities. As a U.S. Treasury-certified Community Development Entity, PCDIC operates with a mission to attract and deploy funds for projects that demonstrably improve quality of life for vulnerable populations and residents of underserved areas.

PCDIC offers three primary financial products: loans to nonprofit organizations and small businesses for community-focused projects, New Markets Tax Credits to incentivize private capital investment in low-income communities, and Community Development Fund grants distributed primarily to nonprofits serving Maricopa County residents. Their lending focuses specifically on projects with measurable social impact rather than purely commercial returns.

What distinguishes PCDIC is its explicit focus on community benefit over profit maximization, its certification status as a Community Development Entity, and its dual approach combining loan products with tax credit mechanisms and grant funding. The organization operates specifically within geographic and demographic boundaries—serving only underserved areas of Phoenix and Maricopa County—rather than offering universal lending access.

PCDIC is best suited for mission-driven organizations rather than traditional commercial borrowers. Prospective applicants should note that eligibility requires alignment with community development goals and location within their service area. The website provides limited detail on loan terms, interest rates, application processes, or funding timelines, requiring direct contact for specific program information.
address: Calvin C. Goode Municipal Building, 251 W Washington St, Phoenix, AZ 85003

company_info.headquarters: Calvin C. Goode Municipal Building, 251 W Washington St, Phoenix, AZ 85003

pros: Certified Community Development Entity by U.S. Treasury, providing credibility and alignment with federal community development standards
Offers multiple funding mechanisms (loans, tax credits, grants) rather than single-product approach
Explicitly targets underserved and low-income communities, addressing capital gaps in economically disadvantaged areas
Serves both nonprofit organizations and small businesses, offering broader eligibility than nonprofit-only lenders
Long operational history since 2002 with backing from City of Phoenix governance
Tax credit offerings provide alternative incentive structures for private capital participation
Geographic focus on Phoenix and Maricopa County creates deep local market knowledge
cons: Website lacks transparent information on loan terms, interest rates, repayment schedules, and approval timelines
Severely limited service area—only underserved areas of Phoenix and Maricopa County, excluding other regions entirely
No clear application process, qualification criteria, or funding amount ranges disclosed on public website
Requires alignment with community development mission, restricting access for purely commercial or for-profit ventures
Minimal contact information or process details visible, creating friction for interested borrowers
best_for: Nonprofit organizations serving vulnerable populations in Phoenix/Maricopa County underserved areas
Small businesses with community development missions in designated low-income communities
Mission-driven entrepreneurs seeking alternatives to traditional commercial lending
Organizations qualified for New Markets Tax Credit incentive structures
diagnosis: PCDIC is best for mission-driven nonprofits and small businesses operating in underserved Phoenix and Maricopa County areas seeking capital with community benefit alignment. The primary caveat is severe geographic limitation combined with lack of transparent public information on loan terms, amounts, and application procedures—requiring direct institutional contact before determining actual eligibility and feasibility.
services: Small business loans for community-focused projects
New Markets Tax Credits for private capital investment incentives
Community Development Fund grants to nonprofits
Community development entity certification and compliance
COVID-19 small business and nonprofit resources and support
